narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, test scores, family background, and other details to forecast your bar performance. For you specifically, it calculated a 96% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of success. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 school grade decile, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school grade decile, all of which pushed the model toward expecting failure rather than success. Most influential was your Year 3 decile ranking at the very bottom (1 out of 10), far below the typical student's mid-range position around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model a significant drop-off in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Next, your LSAT score of 25 stood out as a strong negative signal, well under the average of about 37 seen in students who pass, making the model view it as a key barrier to bar readiness. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom (1 out of 10) compared to the average around the 6th, reinforced this pattern by suggesting early academic challenges that persisted. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher deciles and LSATs in the 35+ range, your profile appeared riskier across these core academic indicators, even with your middle family income bracket aligning with the norm. Full-time enrollment plans, like yours, are common and didn't sway the decision much. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting where your record deviated from patterns of bar passers. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average or higher, while building a stronger academic narrative through additional coursework or explanations for your low deciles in any reapplication. Many students in similar situations improve their profiles and succeed on later attempts, so targeted preparation could shift your odds dramatically next time.
